coredata.dc:title: Study on the fire extinguishing effect of compressed nitrogen foam on 280 Ah lithium iron phosphate battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiaobin
Li, Xinzhe
Li, Chenyao
Wu, Junli
Liu, Boyu
coredata.dc:description: This study conducted experimental analyses on a 280 Ah single lithium iron phosphate battery using an independently constructed experimental platform to assess the efficacy of compressed nitrogen foam in extinguishing lithium-ion battery fires. Based on theoretical analysis, the fire-extinguishing effects of compressed nitrogen foam at different outlet pressures from foam mixture tanks were analyzed, examining factors such as battery surface temperature, flame temperature, and thermal weight loss. The results indicate that the compressed nitrogen foam can extinguish the open flame of the battery in 14 s at 0.7 MPa, with the battery's surface temperature dropping by approximately 11 % before and after the application of the extinguishing agent. Compared with other commonly used extinguishing agents, the compressed nitrogen foam demonstrates superior extinguishing efficiency, but its cooling efficiency is somewhat lower. At pressures ranging from 0.4 to 0.6 MPa, the foam displays prolonged drainage time and sustained cooling effects, rendering it more suitable for lithium-ion battery fire scenarios. To address the issue of reduced cooling performance during later stages of fire suppression by compressed nitrogen foam, an intermittent injection approach has been proposed to effectively preserve its cooling efficacy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium iron phosphate battery
Compressed nitrogen foam
Lithium battery combustion
Fire extinguishing effect
Fire fighting strategy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extinguishing effect of CNF on LFP under 0.7 MPa condition
Extinguishing effect of CNF under different pressures

head.ce:title: Study on the fire extinguishing effect of compressed nitrogen foam on 280 Ah lithium iron phosphate battery

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Fire Protection Engineering Institute, Chinese People's Police University, Langfang, 065000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study conducted experimental analyses on a 280 Ah single lithium iron phosphate battery using an independently constructed experimental platform to assess the efficacy of compressed nitrogen foam in extinguishing lithium-ion battery fires. Based on theoretical analysis, the fire-extinguishing effects of compressed nitrogen foam at different outlet pressures from foam mixture tanks were analyzed, examining factors such as battery surface temperature, flame temperature, and thermal weight loss. The results indicate that the compressed nitrogen foam can extinguish the open flame of the battery in 14 s at 0.7 MPa, with the battery's surface temperature dropping by approximately 11 % before and after the application of the extinguishing agent. Compared with other commonly used extinguishing agents, the compressed nitrogen foam demonstrates superior extinguishing efficiency, but its cooling efficiency is somewhat lower. At pressures ranging from 0.4 to 0.6 MPa, the foam displays prolonged drainage time and sustained cooling effects, rendering it more suitable for lithium-ion battery fire scenarios. To address the issue of reduced cooling performance during later stages of fire suppression by compressed nitrogen foam, an intermittent injection approach has been proposed to effectively preserve its cooling efficacy.

ce:section.ce:para: The question of how to effectively suppress fires in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is a significant challenge hindering their safe application. The unique physicochemical properties of LIB make them susceptible to irreversible thermal runaway reactions, which can lead to fires or even explosions [1]. Lithium iron phosphate battery (LFP), as one of the predominant types of LIBs currently utilized, are extensively employed in energy storage applications due to their enhanced stability. As a result, investigating strategies for extinguishing LFP fires is crucial for enhancing safety standards in energy storage and promoting advancements in this sector.

To address the issue of fire suppression in LIBs, extensive research has been conducted by scholars both domestically and internationally. Notably, studies from the United States and Europe have been at the forefront of this field [2]. The U.S. Federal Aviation Administration [3] evaluated ten common extinguishing agents for their effectiveness in combating LIB fires. Their findings indicated that water-based extinguishing agents exhibit superior efficacy, while gas and dry powder extinguishing agents demonstrate limited effectiveness in suppressing such fires. Meanwhile, the UK Civil Aviation Authority [4] investigated the performance of water-based agents, Halon, FE-36, dry powder extinguishing agents, and fire blankets on typical LIB fires found in portable electronic devices. Their results suggested that Halon and FE-36 were more efficient. Additionally, DEKRA [5] conducted simulation experiments which revealed that water could successfully extinguish electric vehicle LIB fires; however, challenges related to excessive water usage and prolonged extinguishing times were identified. Furthermore, it was discovered that incorporating substances like F-500 and Firesorb could significantly enhance the efficiency of fire suppression for LIB fires [6].

In response to the increasing adoption of LIBs in China driven by national development priorities, extensive research has been conducted by scholars from the State Grid, research institutes, and top universities. Ren et al. [7] investigated the thermal runaway response of LIBs in heptafluoropropane (HFC-227ea) and nitrogen atmospheres. Their findings indicate that HFC-227ea significantly mitigates the initial phase of self-ignition in batteries; however, its effectiveness diminishes during combustion and explosion stages. Wang et al. [8] confirmed the efficacy of HFC-227ea, perfluorohexanone (Novec1230), and carbon dioxide (CO2) in controlling fires in lithium titanate batteries. They observed that both HFC-227ea and Novec1230 effectively extinguish fires; nevertheless, violent internal chain reactions still occur within the batteries leading to potential reignition after cessation of extinguishing agent release. Therefore, they suggest prompt and sustained application of the extinguishing agent to prevent reignition. Sun [9] investigated the use of liquid nitrogen for extinguishing LFP fires, noting that while it can douse visible flames, it fails to impede thermal runaway spread. Various studies demonstrate [10] that dry powders and hot aerosols can suppress open battery fires but do not prevent re-ignition. Geng [11], Jiang [12], and Zhang [13] et al. have observed significant variations in the impact of fine water mist on temperature control during different stages of thermal runaway in LIB. It has been found to be effective at the preinitiation stage but ineffective during the deflagration stage. Therefore, it can be inferred that the rate of temperature rise plays a crucial role in determining suppression effectiveness. When there is a rapid increase in temperature, spraying fine water mist during the preinitiation stage only delays the occurrence of deflagration without preventing thermal runaway. Zhou [14,15] and his research group have demonstrated the potential benefits of incorporating additives into fine water mist for fire suppression purposes. The addition of surfactants as additives reduces water's surface tension, leading to smaller droplet sizes, enhanced evaporation processes, and improved firefighting efficiency. Considering the limitations associated with using a single fire suppressant for LIB fires, numerous researchers have explored collaborative suppression strategies involving diverse extinguishing agents. Zhang et al. [16] conducted experiments to investigate the combined suppression effects of CO2, Novec1230, HFC-227ea, and fine water mist on large-capacity LIB fires, considering firefighting performance, cooling effects, and cost-effectiveness. Their findings suggest that a combination of Novec1230 and fine water mist yields optimal results. Collaborative research by State Grid Jiangsu Electric Power Co., Ltd., theUniversity of Science and Technology of China, and other entities [17] supports the utilization of gas-based agents (Novec1230 and HFC-227ea) in conjunction with fine water mist for extinguishing fires at LFP storage power stations. Meanwhile constructors are advised to select an appropriate system based on factors such as power station size, battery storage layout, potential economic impacts, and acceptable community risk.

The integrated approach to fire suppression combines the advantages of gaseous and fine water mist agents, potentially offering a more efficient solution. However, practical implementation encounters various challenges, including high usage and maintenance costs, as well as limitations in its sole application to initial fire stages in storage power stations. Currently, effective methods for addressing extensive LIB fires remain elusive.

Northern and northwestern China possess abundant solar and wind energy resources, along with a substantial number of energy storage power stations to support new energy generation. However, most of these power stations face water scarcity issues. To address the challenge of fire suppression in these regions, Compressed nitrogen foam (CNF) is proposed as a water-saving fire extinguishing measure. This study employs theoretical analysis and experimental validation to investigate the effectiveness of CNF in suppressing LIB fires, building upon previous research findings. The fire extinguishing effect of CNF on LFP was analyzed based on temperature changes and mass loss before and after the release of the fire suppressant agent, providing valuable insights into effective fire suppression methods for LFP.

The effectiveness of controlling a LFP fire with CNF hinges on the extinguishing agent's ability to penetrate the base of the flame in order to suppress combustion, as well as its capacity to adhere to the surface of combustible materials, thereby providing sustained cooling. Fig. 1 illustrates the extinguishing mechanism. Research conducted by Lang et al. [18] demonstrates that for Class B fires, CNF significantly enhances firefighting efficacy compared to compressed air foam (CAF) by more than 1.6 times, and when compared to suction foam, it exceeds effectiveness by over 2.5 times.

A considerable number of scholars have extensively investigated the thermal runaway and combustion characteristics of LFP. Notably, Ping et al. [19] conducted a comprehensive study on the combustion traits of 50 Ah LFP and proposed that LFP undergo a multistage thermal runaway process often accompanied by jet fires. Consequently, an extinguishing agent with substantial momentum is required. CNF, due to its high volume of compressed nitrogen, imparts the sprayed foam with the necessary momentum to effectively suppress jet fires in LFP.

The key parameters in the combustion process of LIB are the total heat generated and the rate of heat generation, which are determined by the energy conservation equation as stated [20]:
(1)
ρ
C p
∂ T
∂ t
= ∇ ∙
(
k ∇ T
)
+
Q heat
+
Q ele
+
Q rec
+
Q dis

According to equation (1), it is evident that the heat generation in LIB primarily originates from three sources: Qheat, representing the external heat flux mainly consisting of combustion heat feedback; Qele, signifying the electric heat produced by charge transfer and electrochemical reactions during the charge and discharge process; and Qrec, denoting the heat emitted due to thermal runaway reactions within the battery. Said et al. [21] conducted a study on the heat generation characteristics of a 1880 mAh rhombic LIB, revealing that internal thermal runaway resulted in the release of 33 ± 1 kJ of energy, while combustion of battery jet gas contributed to 113 ± 19 kJ of heat generation. These findings imply that Qheat exceeds the combined contributions of Qele and Qrec [21–23]. Extensive experimental data confirm a positive correlation between LIB capacity increase and both total heat production and rate of heat generation.

The LFP system also adheres to Semenov theory, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . When the cooling efficiency of the system surpasses its heat generation efficiency, a decrease in battery temperature occurs. Given that LIB thermal runaway is an ongoing exothermic process, fire suppressants must provide sustained cooling effects. Moreover, due to the exponential increase in combustion heat production rate, the speed of fire suppression directly impacts the heat production efficiency of the combustion system. Therefore, both the cooling efficiency of fire-extinguishing agents and the speed of fire suppression should be integrated as essential metrics for evaluating LIB fire suppressants' performance. In case of successful extinguishment, there will be a sudden decline in LIB's heat production rate. At this time, the fire can be successfully extinguished as long as the cooling efficiency of the inhibitor is higher than the combined thermal runaway of the reaction inside the battery and heat release efficiency of the electrochemical reaction.

The primary cooling mechanism of CNF is attributed to the water it contains, which undergoes predominantly convective heat transfer during the foam's injection phase. This process adheres to Newton's law of cooling, which can be expressed as equation (2).
(2)
q x ″
= − h Δ T
Where h is the convective heat transfer coefficient, which increases as the fluid flow rate rises.

During the foam-covered cooling phase, heat conduction becomes the predominant mode of cooling. At this stage, the efficiency of cooling provided by the CNF relies primarily on the foam's drainage capacity. Faezian et al. [24] have further advanced the standard drainage equation and simplified it into a first-order model (equations (3)–(6)), which accurately characterizes free drainage.
(3)
A
(
z , t
)
=
A 0
{
(
z
U 0
t
)
1 m
z ≤
U 0
t
1
z ≥
U 0
t
(4)
ε
(
z , t
)
=
ε 0
{
(
z vt
)
1 m
z ≤ vt
1
z ≥ vt
(5)
V
( t )
V
( f )
=
{
1
m + 1
(
vt h
)
vt ≤ h
1 −
m
m + 1
(
h vt
)
1 m
vt ≥ h
(6)
U 0
= 2 ρ g
A 0
/
μ *
In the framework of this model, ‘A(z,t)’ represents the cross-sectional area of the plateau border channels within the foam, which varies with both the vertical position ‘z’ and time ‘t’. The term ‘ℇ(z,t)’ denotes the local liquid volume fraction in the foam, which also depends on ‘z’ and ‘t’. The variable ‘U0’ specifies the maximum characteristic velocity within the system. The parameter m takes a value of m = 1 for Plateau border dominated flow and m = 1/2 for vertex dominated flow with dissipative shear flow at junctions. The symbols ‘ρ’, ‘g’, and ‘μ*’ refer to fluid density, acceleration due to gravity, and fluid viscosity respectively.

Revised by Kang [25], the original equation was enhanced to incorporate the temperature impact on foam liquid viscosity, and the drainage equation for foam under varying temperatures was modified accordingly (equations (7)–(9)). The relationship between temperature and foam viscosity was established through experimental fitting.
(7)
V
( t )
V
( f )
=
{
1
m + 1
(
V T
t
h
)
vt ≤ h
1 −
m
m + 1
(
h
V T
t
)
1 m
vt ≥ h
(8)
V T
=
U 0
(
m + 1
)
ε 0 m
(9)
μ *
= 4.928 − 0.043 T + 1.388
T 2
The fitting results demonstrated a high level of accuracy with an R2 value of 0.998 obtained for the empirical equation (8).

The viscosity of foam in practical applications is influenced by various factors, with temperature being a primary determinant. To simplify the problem, based on the aforementioned model, the key factors impacting the cooling efficiency of foam are identified as follows: water content within the foam and combustion zone temperature. Consequently, it can be predicted that elevated temperatures will accelerate the drainage rate of foam, leading to rapid precipitation of water from the foam. As a result of continuous water precipitation, the extinguishing agent's cooling capacity gradually weakens until it eventually diminishes.

In addition to the aforementioned fire extinguishing mechanisms, when CNF is applied onto combustible materials’ surfaces, it forms a protective layer that effectively isolates the surface from surrounding air. However, significant amounts of flammable vapors are emitted during a LIB thermal runaway event. This foam layer cannot prevent the generation of these vapors; thus, the insulating mechanism of CNF provides limited effectiveness in suppressing individual battery fires. Nevertheless, this layer also acts as a barrier against radiant heat transfer, which may be crucial in scenarios involving multiple batteries undergoing thermal runaway events. Consequently, further investigation is warranted to assess the efficacy of CNF in extinguishing LIB fires.

The schematic layout of the experimental apparatus is depicted in Fig. 3 . The LFP is centrally positioned within the combustion chamber, which is situated above a mass measurement device. Thermocouples labeled TC1, TC2, and TC3 are placed at precise distances of 10 cm, 15 cm, and 20 cm respectively from the safety valve of the battery to measure flame temperatures. On the opposite side of the battery, thermocouple TC4 is installed to monitor the temperature of the heating element. Adjacent to the cathode of the battery, thermocouple TC5 is deployed to assess its core temperature. Additionally, diagonally arranged from upper-left to lower-right (5 cm from periphery) on the face of the battery are thermocouples TC6, TC7, and TC8 used for determining surface temperature. Voltage leads are attached to record voltage fluctuations during combustion trials. The gas detection apparatus intake is mounted 20 cm above the positive pole of the battery while introducing CNF spout at its upper-left corner.

During the experimental procedure, a type K armored thermocouple with a 1 mm diameter probe (model WRNK-191-BO2P) was utilized for temperature measurements ranging from 0 to 1300 °C. Data acquisition was facilitated by a Fluke 2638a data logger, capable of real-time display and recording at a frequency of 1 Hz on a computer. The thermocouples, in conjunction with the Fluke 2638a data logger, constituted the system's temperature monitoring component. The voltage detection system was established through integration of the Fluke 2638a data logger with a multimeter. The stainless steel heating plate had dimensions of 250 mm × 250 mm and operated at a voltage of 220 V with power consumption of 0.6 KW, providing heat resistance up to temperatures as high as 1400 °C. Mass changes were precisely tracked using a Nanjing Bonita DNE series electronic balance featuring an accuracy level of ±0.1 g and data acquisition rate of 8 Hz. Gaseous emission analysis was conducted using an MS400-4-D portable multi-gas detector (monitoring CO2, CO, EX, and O2 levels), which transmitted data to a PC via RS485 signal transmission. This detector exhibited T90 response time less than 20 s and possessed IP67 rating for protection.
The CNF system utilizes a pushcart-type foam fire extinguishing device with a 200 L capacity and a working pressure range of 0.2–0.9 MPa, maintaining a gas-liquid ratio of 6:1.
The experimental setup employed a square aluminum shell LFP with a capacity of 280 Ah, operating at 3.2 V. It featured dimensions of 30 mm in thickness, 135 mm in width, and 220 mm in length.

Fully charge the experimental LFP (100 %) using the designated charger and allow it to rest for 12 h to ensure stabilization. Secure the LFP in the combustion chamber with a fixed fixture, and install various instruments including temperature sensor, voltage meter, mass measurement instrument, gas analyzer, video camera, and CNF fire suppression system. Conduct a comprehensive check to confirm proper functioning of all components.

The thermal runaway in the LFP was initiated by subjecting it to an external heat source with a heating power of 600 W. The temperature of the battery continued to rise due to the combined influence of the external heat source and its own exothermic reaction. Once it reached the predetermined temperature, the safety valve was triggered to release combustible gases. These combustible gases were ignited using an electric spark lighter. After that, the external heat source was discontinued, allowing for a pre-combustion period of 3 min for the battery. Following completion of pre-combustion, according to experimental design pressure and flow specifications, a downward spray of 30 L CNF consisting of a 3 % AFFF foam mixture was directed towards the combustion vessel at an angle of 45°. Various data including extinguishing time, temperature measurements at individual points, and other relevant experimental data were recorded by experimental equipment. Six separate experimental trials were conducted following specific protocols outlined in Table 1 below.

Following the completion of the fire suppression test, the previously combusting LFP was extracted from the test setup, thoroughly dried, and subsequently weighed. It was then set aside for a 24 h observation period to monitor any indications of re-ignition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extinguishing effect of CNF on LFP under 0.7 MPa condition
ce:section.ce:para: The entire experimental sequence is illustrated in Fig. 4 (a–f), depicting the progression from thermal runaway initiation in the LFP to the cessation of fire extinguishing agent spraying. Initially, at 0 s, the battery temperature gradually rises due to continuous exposure to heat from a heating plate. This heating triggers various reactions within the battery, including high-temperature-induced capacity degradation, breakdown of Solid-Electrolyte Interphase (SEI) membrane, interactions between anode and electrolyte, separator melting leading to internal short-circuits, and reactions between cathode and electrolytel. These reactions collectively accelerate the temperature increase of the LFP, propelling it towards an irreversible state of thermal runaway. Concurrently, both temperature elevation and gases released from internal battery reactions cause a continuous rise in internal pressure. At 36 min 39 s, when reaching a critical threshold level, this internal pressure triggers the safety valve release mechanism resulting in a discharge of abundant white smoke accompanied by a distinct popping noise. After 10 s, a predetermined ignition device activates igniting flammable gases and generating a significant flame that reaches up to 1 m in height. The flame subsequently decreases in height, stabilizing at approximately 0.1 m. 3 min after the pre-ignition event, precisely at 39 min 48 s, the application of the fire suppression agent commences. Upon introduction of the suppression agent, there is a rapid decline in battery fire intensity. Merely 14 s later, at 40 min 02 s, the open flames are extinguished. At this juncture, half of the combustion container is filled with CNF while continuing to spray the extinguishing agent. Another 6 s elapse until 40 min 08 s when the suppression agent thoroughly inundates the combustion apparatus and completely envelops the battery. Subsequently, spraying of the suppression agent ceases. Despite no visible flames remaining, white smoke continues to emit from the battery for an additional duration of 810 s. The battery status was depicted in Fig. 5 (a and b) both before and after the experiment.

The variations in battery temperature and voltage throughout the experimental process are illustrated in Fig. 6 (a and b). Fig. 6(a) depicts the complete experimental procedure, while Fig. 6(b) provides a localized magnification of the self-accelerating reaction stage and the extinguishing stage. It can be observed that both core temperature and battery surface temperature gradually increase during the passive heating phase due to heat conduction until the safety valve ruptures. At the moment of safety valve rupture, a significant outflow of smoke occurs, which carries away a portion of heat resulting in a slight decrease in battery temperature. During the self-accelerating reaction stage, an additional temperature increase is observed in the battery due to radiant heat emitted from the flames. This elevation continues until reaching peak temperatures, with the center surface of the battery reaching approximately 118 °C and the core temperature around 75 °C. Subsequently, a plateau in temperature is observed, maintaining the center surface at roughly 115 °C and the core at approximately 72 °C. In the Extinguishing stage, a continuous spray of extinguishing agent consistently reduces both center surface and core temperatures of the battery. The successful extinguishment of the battery fire occurs at time point 2402 s, with a recorded center surface temperature of 109 °C at that moment. Following this event, during the submerged cooling stage, a gradual decline in battery temperatures is observed followed by stabilization over time. The center surface temperature settles around 63 °C and the core temperature around 60 °C. According to equation (10), there is an approximate drop of about 11 % in center surface temperature before and after release of extinguishing agent.
(10)
δ
c o l
=
T a
−
T b
T a
Where,
δ
c o l
is the cooling rate, Ta represents the temperature before the extinguishing agent is released, and Tb represents the temperature after the extinguishing agent is released. Throughout the entire experimental process, a gradual decrease in battery voltage is observed until it reaches zero. Notably, a more pronounced decline in voltage occurs during the submerged cooling phase.

The flame temperature of the battery during the experiment is depicted in Fig. 7 . The data demonstrate that the flame temperature exhibits constant fluctuations, influenced by external environmental factors and various parameters. During ignition, there is a rapid surge in flame temperature, resulting in the formation of a jet-like fire. At this point, the flame reaches a height of nearly 1 m with its highest measured temperature soaring to 786 °C, located 30 cm above the safety valve. As the self-accelerating reaction stage progresses, the flame stabilizes and maintains a height of approximately 0.1 m. At this stage, the peak temperature recorded at a measurement point situated 10 cm above the safety valve is 729 °C. In subsequent extinguishing stages, continuous application of an extinguishing agent causes a sharp decline in battery flame temperature. Upon complete extinguishment, the measured temperature at a distance of 10 cm above the safety valve drops to approximately 370 °C.

The temperature rise rates for both the core and surface of LFP throughout the experimental process are illustrated in Fig. 8 (a–d). During the passive heating stage, there is a positive but relatively low rate of increase in both core and surface temperatures. This rate dramatically escalates at ignition and remains high during the self-accelerating reaction stage. This pattern suggests that, during combustion of LFP, heat feedback from flames significantly exceeds the heat generated by thermal runaway within the battery itself. As the extinguishing stage begins, the rates of temperature rise shift to negative values. At the moment of extinguishing agent release, there is a surge in cooling rate experienced by the battery which then gradually decreases and stabilizes over time. Fig. 8(b) and (d) demonstrate that during the submerged cooling stage, there is a gradual increase in temperature rise rate indicating a diminishing cooling effect of CNF as time progresses. This observation aligns with theoretical projections outlined in Equations (7)–(9).

The transformations in mass and mass loss rate during the experimental process are depicted in Fig. 9 (a and b). The presented data indicate that the battery's mass remains stable during the passive heating stage. An event is observed at 632 s when the safety valve of the battery opens, resulting in a significant release of white smoke and causing an abrupt reduction in mass by approximately 131 g, with a corresponding mass loss rate of 40.87 g/s. Throughout the self-accelerating reaction stage, extinguishing stage, and submerged cooling stage, there is a persistent decrease in the battery's mass. The self-accelerating reaction stage exhibits a higher rate of mass loss compared to subsequent stages, such as the Extinguishing and submerged cooling stages. This suggests that the release of CNF significantly mitigates the thermal runaway reaction rate within the LFP, thereby reducing gas production. The intense thermal runaway reaction within LFP is known to continuously generate gases. Additionally, the continuous rise in temperature leads to electrolyte evaporation. Upon opening of the battery's safety valve, the ongoing discharge of these gases results in a persistent decrease in battery mass. Therefore, monitoring the rate of mass loss can serve as an indicative benchmark for assessing the severity of internal thermal runaway reactions occurring within batteries. Notably, after 2786 s, there is stabilization in battery mass, indicating that by this stage it is likely that internal thermal runaway reactions have ceased.

After conducting a comparative analysis using experimental data that closely matched the parameters employed in this study, the fire suppression efficiency and cooling capacity of different fire extinguishing agents were assessed and compared, as presented in Table 2 . The findings indicate that CNF exhibits superior fire extinguishing efficiency compared to other agents, albeit with a relatively lower cooling capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extinguishing effect of CNF under different pressures
ce:section.ce:para: The foaming ratio and drainage time are crucial parameters for assessing foam properties[28]. The foaming ratio determines the density of the foam, with larger ratios resulting in smaller and more uniform foam sizes. On the other hand, the stability of the foam is determined by its drainage time. Fig. 10 illustrates how pressure influences the performance parameters of CNF. It can be observed that when the pressure is below 0.4 MPa, the foaming ratio gradually increases with increasing pressure. However, when the pressure exceeds 0.4 MPa, there is a slight decrease in foaming ratio which then stabilizes at a relatively constant level. In this scenario, it is primarily the drainage time that governs foam properties. When operating at pressures lower than 0.6 MPa, an increase in pressure leads to an increase in drainage time for CNF until it reaches its peak value at 0.6 MPa. Subsequently, further increases in pressure cause a significant decline in drainage time followed by stabilization at a low level. Therefore, it can be concluded that within the range of 0.4–0.6 MPa pressures, foams exhibit enhanced continuous cooling capabilities.

The changes in battery temperature when a LFP fire is extinguished by CNF under different pressures are illustrated in Fig. 11 (a and b). Overall, the application of CNF at varying pressures effectively cools the battery within 20 s and continues to cool it during the immersion cooling stage. Taking the example of battery core temperature, an evident cooling node can be observed at 15 s, 15 s, 9 s, 10 s, and 6 s with increasing pressure. Within the first 20 s of extinguishing agent discharge, the temperature of the battery core decreases by respective values of 6.5 °C, 3.6 °C, 7.9 °C, 10.3 °C and 21.3 °C. This corresponds to cooling rates of approximately 5.2 %, 3 %, 8.4 %, 9 % and 17 %. This is attributed to the positive correlation between the pressure of the CNF and its flow rate, resulting in enhanced convective heat transfer capacity during extinguishing agent injection as depicted by equation (2), thereby leading to an escalated cooling rate throughout the extinguishing process. After releasing the extinguishing agent for approximately 100 s, it was observed that the cooling rate of CNF weakened at a pressure of 0.4 MPa and even caused an increase in temperature at a pressure of 0.2 MPa within the battery core. This phenomenon suggests that during the extinguishing process, CNF may have insufficient cooling capacity at pressures of both 0.2 MPa and 0.4 MPa to handle sudden temperature increases during thermal runaway events in LFP. However, when tested under pressures of 0.6 MPa, 0.7 MPa, and 0.8 MPa throughout the entire fire suppression and cooling process, no significant rise in temperature occurred indicating that CNF's cooling rate remained higher than heat release rates from LFP.

The change in flame temperature during the extinguishment of LFP fire by CNF under different pressures is illustrated in Fig. 12 . It can be observed that at 0.2 MPa and 0.4 MPa, the flame temperature exhibits a gradual decline with no distinct cooling node within the first 15 s after injection of the extinguishing agent; instead, there is an evident rebound phenomenon in the flame temperature. Subsequently, after 15 s, the flame gradually diminishes as a result of foam isolation effect rather than solely due to its cooling effect. Under conditions of 0.6 MPa and 0.7 MPa, a clear cooling node occurs at 10 s and 14 s respectively, following which the flame temperature decreases linearly. Under the conditions of 0.8 MPa and 0.9 MPa, the cooling rate of the foam is further enhanced, leading to a distinct cooling node in flame temperature at 5 s and 7 s respectively, resulting in a rapid decline in temperature and extinguishment of the flame.

Fig. 13 (a and b) illustrates the suppressive effect of CNF on LFP's thermal runaway under varying pressures, based on the mass change of CNF during extinguishing and comparison of mass loss post-extinguishing. The intensity of thermal runaway reaction inside the battery increases with greater battery mass loss. The quality of LFP remains stable until safety valve opening, as shown in Fig. 9(a). For clarity, we consider safety valve opening as time node 0 s in Fig. 13(a). At this point, battery quality sharply declines and continues to decrease during pre-combustion stage. Upon release of fire extinguishing agent, higher pressure results in stronger cooling effect and better suppression of LFP's thermal runaway within a short period. However, during the submerged cooling stage, the mass loss rate of LFP exhibits a slower trend at 0.2 and 0.4 MPa pressures. Fig. 13(b) illustrates the comparison of LFP mass loss after the complete fire extinguishing process. The results demonstrate that at 0.2 MPa pressure, both the cooling rate and continuous cooling capacity of the extinguishing agent are relatively low, resulting in maximum mass loss. Conversely, at 0.4 MPa pressure, although the cooling rate of the extinguishing agent remains slightly insufficient, its continuous cooling capacity is robust, leading to minimal battery mass loss as well. This observation indicates that for effectively suppressing thermal runaway in LIB, long-term continuous cooling capacity holds greater significance than short-term rapid cooling capability does. As for pressures ranging from 0.6 to 0.8 MPa, an increase in CNF pressure leads to a decrease in foam's continuous cooling capacity and subsequently causes an elevated battery mass loss rate. Therefore, when CNF is applied within a range of 0.4–0.6 MPa pressure levels, it demonstrates superior inhibition against thermal runaway occurrence in LFP.

The present study primarily investigates the fire suppression efficacy of CNF on LFP fires under varying pressures. By integrating experimental findings with existing research, the outcomes reveal the fire extinguishing performance of CNF on 280 Ah monomer LFP as follows.
(1)
The fire suppression mechanism of CNF on LFP primarily encompasses cooling and isolation effects. The application of CNF at 0.7 MPa effectively extinguishes the open flame of the battery within 14 s, resulting in a reduction of approximately 11 % in the battery's surface temperature upon agent deployment. In comparison to conventional fire suppressants, CNF exhibits superior fire suppression efficiency while demonstrating relatively lower cooling efficacy.
(2)
In order to suppress thermal runaway of LIB, the long-term continuous cooling capability is more crucial than short-term rapid cooling ability. When the pressure ranges from 0.4 to 0.6 MPa, the foam generated exhibits a longer liquid evolution time, stronger continuous cooling capacity, and relatively effective fire control. However, when the pressure exceeds 0.6 MPa, although the cooling rate of CNF during fire extinguishing stage continuously improves with increasing pressure, its performance deteriorates in terms of continuous cooling effect during immersion cooling stage due to poor foam characteristics. Therefore, CNF at a pressure range of 0.4–0.6 MPa demonstrates superior inhibition against thermal runaway in LFP.
(3)
In the immersed cooling stage, due to the inherent characteristics of the foam itself and the reduction of the temperature difference between the battery and the foam extinguishing agent, the cooling effect of the foam will gradually decline with the passage of time, and even the problem of insufficient cooling efficiency will occur. Therefore, it is suggested that intermittent injection can be adopted in the practical application process. The foam fluid in the fire site is constantly updated to maintain the greater cooling efficiency of the CNF.
(4)
This study demonstrates the potential use of CNF as an auxiliary technical approach to effectively delay the spread of fire during the initial stage of LIB fires in energy storage power stations. This enriches the existing technical system for suppressing LIB fires and provides valuable insights for designing and conducting experiments on LIB fire suppression. Further investigation is required to understand the relationship between the microscopic thermal runaway process of LFP and the macroscopic cooling effect achieved through CNF injection. Additionally, it is necessary to explore the fire extinguishing efficacy of CNF at both module and storage tank levels for LFP.

Xiaobin Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xinzhe Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Chenyao Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Data curation. Junli Wu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Boyu Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research presented in this study was financially supported by the Hebei Provincial Key Research Project 22375408D, titled “Investigation and Development of a Fixed Compressed Air Foam Fire Extinguishing System for Extra High Voltage Transformers.”
